metadata.title: Why the Tumor Genome Is a Fossil Record of What the Cell Encountered
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL=preserved pattern/correct signature/intact repair, CRIMSON=DNA damage/adduct/broken sequence
metadata.note: Exclusions: no full chemical mechanism for all carcinogens (only benzo[a]pyrene and UV in detail); no COSMIC signature database details; no procarcinogen activation enzyme kinetics; no aflatoxin-HBV interaction (separate card); no linear no-threshold model for radiation.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Pragmatist register (voices/pragmatist/VOICE.md + HAI.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the HAI outro from AUTHOR.MD :: Humanitarians AI
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

beats.narration_text: A 2025 sequencing report names what was inside a lung cell in 1985. The mutation pattern is still there.
beats.new_visual_element: [TITLE] Why the Tumor Genome Is a Fossil Record

beats.narration_text: The report shows 340 mutations, dominant pattern G-to-T transversions at specific trinucleotide contexts. An oncologist identifies the exposure before reading the clinical history.
beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] sequencing report panel with G->T dominant pattern, Signature 4 chip

beats.narration_text: Each carcinogen leaves a distinctive mutation pattern that persists for decades. The mechanism: how does a chemical write a permanent fingerprint into DNA?
beats.new_visual_element: [QUESTION] gap formula on screen: pattern persists decades -- why?

beats.narration_text: Mutations are heritable changes in the DNA sequence. Cell division copies every altered base exactly into each daughter cell.
beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] cell division tree: G->T mutation inherited across generations

beats.narration_text: A chemical hitting one cell once might produce one random mutation — invisible in a genome of millions of letters. Pattern requires a non-random mechanism.
beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] genome grid: one crimson G among teal letters

beats.narration_text: Benzo[a]pyrene does not hit randomly. Enzymes convert it to BPDE, which attacks guanine at specific trinucleotide motifs — the same sequence contexts, repeatedly.

props.headline: But benzo-a-pyrene doesn't hit randomly. Enzymes convert it to BPDE --…

beats.new_visual_element: [STILL geo] benzo[a]pyrene -> BPDE -> guanine adduct -> G->T chain

beats.narration_text: The BPDE adduct distorts the helix. DNA polymerase cannot read through it accurately and inserts adenine opposite the damaged guanine — a predictable misincorporation.
beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] DNA ladder: adduct on G, kinked rung, polymerase inserts A (wrong)

beats.narration_text: After one more replication round, that mismatch becomes a fixed G-to-T transversion — permanent, heritable, copied into every daughter cell from that point forward.
beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] two daughter cells: one G:C (teal), one T:A (crimson, fixed mutation)

beats.narration_text: UV operates differently. A photon fuses adjacent pyrimidines into a cyclobutane dimer. Polymerase inserts adenine opposite both — producing C-to-T transitions specifically at dipyrimidine sites.
beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] UV dimer formation: C-C + photon -> C=C dimer -> C->T transition

beats.narration_text: Repeated exposure multiplies these context-specific errors. The pattern accumulates proportionally — stronger with each pack-year, each hour of UV. The fingerprint grows with dose.
beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] bar chart growing: G->T bars at GpC context rising over time

beats.narration_text: Four carcinogens produce four distinct patterns. Tobacco: G-to-T transversions. UV: C-to-T at dipyrimidines. Aflatoxin: a sharp spike at TP53 codon 249. Mismatch-repair deficiency: hundreds of mutations, evenly distributed.

props.headline: Four carcinogens, four fingerprints. Tobacco: G-to-T. UV: C-to-T at dipyrimidines. Aflatoxin:…

beats.new_visual_element: [STILL geo] four mutational signature bar charts side-by-side

beats.narration_text: Limit: the signature names the carcinogen mechanism, not the specific exposure event. A never-smoker with the tobacco signature indicates secondary exposure the clinical history missed — the genome is a more reliable witness than recall.
beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] two-column: genome says tobacco signature / file says never-smoker

beats.narration_text: Four tumor genomes. Each from a different patient. The mutation pattern identifies the exposure before the clinical file is opened.
beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] four empty genome boxes -- illustrative example setup

beats.narration_text: Melanoma: C-to-T, UV. Lung adenocarcinoma: G-to-T, tobacco. Liver tumor: codon 249 spike, aflatoxin. Colon cancer: 400 mutations, uniform — MMR deficiency. Four patterns, four histories. Illustrative.
beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] four illustrated tumor genome cards with pattern and carcinogen labels (illustrative)

beats.narration_text: Each carcinogen produces adducts at specific sequence contexts. Polymerase misreads them with context-specific bias. The pattern is fixed and heritable — a chemical fingerprint encoded in DNA that persists indefinitely.
beats.new_visual_element: [ENDCARD] CANCER BIOLOGY eyebrow, recap: fingerprint in DNA

beats.name: OutroSeries
beats.narration_text: This is part of the Cancer Biology series from Humanitarians AI.

props.tagline: AI education for humanitarian and social-impact practitioners.
